learning_objectives: Understand the historical evolution of media theory from print to digital
Identify key theorists and their contributions to media studies
Analyze how media shapes and is shaped by culture
Apply foundational theoretical frameworks to contemporary media examples
readings: Understanding Media by Marshall McLuhan, Ch. 1
Media & Culture by Campbell, Martin, and Fabos, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Media Studies. This course examines how media systems operate, how audiences interact with media content, and how media practitioners can create ethical, effective content across platforms. Media is not neutral -- every medium shapes the messages it carries, every platform incentivizes certain content over others, and every audience member brings their own context to interpretation. Understanding these dynamics is essential for anyone who creates, distributes, or consumes media professionally. We begin with the foundational theorists whose ideas still frame how we understand media today. Marshall McLuhan argued that the medium itself matters more than the content it carries -- television changed society not because of what was on television but because television existed. Stuart Hall showed that audiences do not passively receive messages but actively interpret them through their own cultural lens. Henry Jenkins demonstrated that modern audiences are not just consumers but participants who create, remix, and distribute content. These ideas are not historical curiosities -- they are the analytical tools you will use throughout this course to understand everything from TikTok algorithms to political propaganda to brand storytelling. Media theory gives you the vocabulary and frameworks to see what others miss: the invisible structures that determine what gets communicated, to whom, and with what effect.
key_concepts: McLuhan's 'The medium is the message': the technology of communication shapes society more than the content communicated through it
Stuart Hall's encoding/decoding model: producers encode meaning; audiences decode through dominant (intended), negotiated (partial), or oppositional (contrary) readings
Uses and gratifications theory: audiences actively choose media to satisfy specific needs (information, entertainment, social connection, identity)
The Frankfurt School and critical media theory: media as ideology, cultural industry, and manufactured consent
Henry Jenkins' convergence culture: the collision of old and new media, participatory culture, and collective intelligence
Technological determinism vs social construction: whether technology drives social change or society shapes how technology is adopted
Media ecology: studying media as environments that shape perception, understanding, feeling, and value
The public sphere (Habermas): media's role in facilitating democratic discourse and how digital platforms alter it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of the same news story across print, broadcast TV, Twitter/X, and TikTok, illustrating how the medium shapes the message in each platform's constraints and affordances
setup_requirements: Access to university library databases (JSTOR, Communication Abstracts)
Social media accounts for observation exercises
Note-taking setup for media diary assignment

review.value: The theory that media technology is the primary driver of social change, independent of content. McLuhan's hot/cool media distinction and the idea that the printing press created nationalism are examples.

Stuart Hall's model: producers encode preferred meaning into media texts, but audiences decode through dominant reading (accepting intended meaning), negotiated (partially accepting), or oppositional (rejecting and substituting alternative meaning).

Audiences are not passive receivers but active selectors who choose media to fulfill needs: information (news), personal identity (lifestyle content), social integration (community), entertainment (escape).
Henry Jenkins' concept where old and new media collide, audiences become participants rather than spectators, and media flows across multiple platforms driven by both producers and consumers.

class_schedule.topic: The Digital Media Landscape and Platform Economics
learning_objectives: Map the current digital media ecosystem including platforms, creators, and distributors
Understand platform business models and their influence on content
Analyze the shift from broadcast to algorithmic content distribution
Evaluate the concept of the attention economy and its societal implications
readings: Platform Capitalism by Nick Srnicek, Ch. 2
Algorithms of Oppression by Safiya Noble, Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: The media landscape has fundamentally shifted from a broadcast model with few creators and many consumers to a networked model where anyone can publish but platforms control distribution. This shift has profound implications for media practitioners, audiences, and society. Platforms like YouTube, Instagram, TikTok, and X are not neutral pipes -- they are businesses whose revenue depends on capturing and monetizing user attention. Their algorithms determine what content gets seen, by whom, and in what context. Understanding platform economics is essential because these incentive structures directly shape what content gets created, what goes viral, and what disappears. Today we map the current digital media ecosystem: who the major platforms are, how they make money, how their algorithms work at a conceptual level, and what incentives these systems create for content creators and consumers. We examine the attention economy framework that treats human attention as a scarce commodity to be captured and sold, and we evaluate its psychological and societal implications. We also introduce the concept of algorithmic bias -- how platform algorithms can amplify harmful content, reinforce existing inequalities, and create filter bubbles that polarize audiences.
key_concepts: Platform economics: network effects, two-sided markets (users + advertisers), data extraction as business model
Algorithmic curation vs editorial curation: automated personalization vs human selection, and the biases inherent in each
Creator economy: monetization models for content creators (ads, subscriptions, patronage, sponsorship, merchandise)
Walled gardens vs open web: content portability, platform lock-in, and the implications for creator independence
The attention economy: human attention as scarce commodity, platform competition for engagement time, and psychological manipulation
Filter bubbles and echo chambers: algorithmic reinforcement of existing preferences reducing exposure to diverse perspectives
Platform power: content moderation decisions, algorithmic amplification, and de-platforming as exercises of quasi-governmental authority
Data privacy and surveillance capitalism: how platforms monetize user data and the implications for individual autonomy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the distribution path of a piece of content from creation through platform algorithms to audience consumption, identifying each stakeholder's incentives at every stage
setup_requirements: Access to platform analytics examples (YouTube Studio, Instagram Insights)

review.value: A platform becomes more valuable as more users join, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. Facebook with 3 billion users is more useful than a new social network with 1,000, creating an almost insurmountable barrier for competitors.

Framework treating human attention as scarce commodity that media platforms compete to capture and monetize. Revenue = attention captured x advertising rate, incentivizing engagement maximization over user wellbeing.

Platform algorithms trained on historical data can amplify existing biases: showing certain job ads to certain demographics, recommending increasingly extreme content to maintain engagement, suppressing marginalized voices.

Shoshana Zuboff's concept: the extraction and commodification of personal data to predict and influence behavior, where the product is not the platform but the behavioral predictions sold to advertisers.

class_schedule.topic: Audience Analysis and Media Consumption Patterns
learning_objectives: Apply quantitative and qualitative methods to analyze media audiences
Distinguish between demographics, psychographics, and behavioral segmentation
Evaluate audience research methodologies and their limitations
Build research-based audience personas for media strategy
readings: Audience Analysis by Denis McQuail, Ch. 3-4
Spreadable Media by Henry Jenkins, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding your audience is the difference between content that resonates and content that disappears. Today we learn the frameworks and tools for knowing who you are reaching, why they engage, and what motivates their media consumption choices. Audience analysis has evolved dramatically from the broadcast era (Nielsen ratings measuring how many households tuned in) to the digital era (granular behavioral tracking measuring not just what people watch but when they pause, rewind, skip, and abandon). Modern audience analysis combines quantitative data (analytics platforms measuring behavior at scale) with qualitative understanding (interviews, focus groups, ethnographic observation revealing motivations and contexts). We build audience personas that synthesize both data types into actionable profiles: fictional but research-based representations of key audience segments that guide content strategy decisions. The critical skill is distinguishing between who your audience IS (observable behavior and demographics) and why they engage (motivations, values, unmet needs) -- because the 'why' is what enables you to create content that genuinely serves them rather than just reaching them.
key_concepts: Demographics (age, gender, location, income) vs psychographics (values, attitudes, lifestyle, personality) vs behavioral segmentation (usage patterns, engagement frequency, content preferences)
Nielsen ratings, ComScore, and digital analytics: how audience measurement evolved from sampling to census-level digital tracking
Qualitative methods: focus groups (group dynamics), interviews (depth), ethnographic observation (natural behavior), surveys (scale)
Audience personas: research-based fictional profiles combining demographic data, behavioral patterns, motivations, goals, and media consumption habits
Digital analytics: session duration, bounce rate, scroll depth, click-through rate, engagement rate -- what each actually measures and its limitations
Social listening: monitoring social media conversations to understand audience sentiment, trending topics, and unmet content needs
Audience fragmentation: the shift from mass audiences to niche communities and its implications for content strategy
Media consumption trends: multi-screening, binge consumption, parasocial relationships, and short-form content preference
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an audience persona using anonymized Google Analytics data and social media insights: combining quantitative metrics with qualitative interview findings into an actionable profile

review.value: Dividing audiences by psychological characteristics: values, opinions, interests, attitudes, lifestyle. Two 35-year-olds may share demographics but have completely different media preferences based on psychographics.

A research-based fictional profile representing a key segment: 'Sarah, 28, urban professional, values authenticity, consumes media primarily on mobile during commute, follows 3 podcasts and prefers long-form written analysis over video.'
Total meaningful interactions (likes, comments, shares, saves) divided by reach or followers. More meaningful than follower count because it measures active response rather than passive following.
One-sided emotional connections audiences form with media figures. Critical for understanding creator economy dynamics where audience loyalty is personal, not institutional.

learning_objectives: Apply narrative structure principles to different media formats
Understand transmedia storytelling and world-building strategies
Adapt stories for platform-specific constraints and audience expectations
Design content that leverages each platform's unique affordances
readings: Convergence Culture by Henry Jenkins, Ch. 3
Story by Robert McKee, Ch. 2-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Every medium has its own grammar for storytelling. A story told on TikTok differs fundamentally from the same story told as a podcast or documentary -- not just in length but in structure, pacing, emotional arc, and audience relationship. A TikTok story must hook in the first second, deliver value in 60 seconds, and invite engagement through comments. A podcast story can build slowly over 45 minutes because the audience has committed their attention. A documentary can use visual evidence and interview testimony over 90 minutes to build a layered argument. Understanding these platform grammars allows you to create content that feels native rather than repurposed. Today we also examine transmedia storytelling: the practice of distributing different story elements across multiple platforms where each medium contributes something unique. The Marvel Cinematic Universe is the canonical example -- films tell the central narrative, Disney+ series explore character backstories, comics fill timeline gaps, and social media extends the community experience. Each platform adds value that could not exist on another, and together they create a story world richer than any single medium could contain.
key_concepts: Three-act structure adaptation: from 30-second reel (compressed setup-conflict-resolution) to feature film (expanded with subplots and character arcs)
Transmedia storytelling: extending narratives across platforms where each medium adds unique value, not mere adaptation
Platform-native storytelling: vertical video (TikTok/Reels), thread narratives (X/Threads), interactive stories (Instagram), audio narratives (podcasts)
World-building: creating story universes that support multiple entry points, character perspectives, and temporal periods
The hero's journey adapted for interactive and participatory media where the audience influences the story
Hook design: the first 1-3 seconds that determine whether a viewer continues or scrolls away on algorithmic platforms
Serial vs episodic storytelling: ongoing narratives with continuity vs self-contained stories with recurring characters
User-generated narrative: stories that emerge from audience participation, fan fiction, community lore, and co-creation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study of the Marvel Cinematic Universe as transmedia storytelling: mapping how story elements distribute across films, series, comics, and social media, each contributing unique narrative value
setup_requirements: Access to streaming platforms for case study clips

review.value: Distributing story elements across multiple platforms where each makes a distinct contribution. Not adaptation (same story, different format) but expansion (different stories, different formats, same world).

Content designed for a specific platform's constraints and affordances: vertical video for TikTok (immersive, swipeable), threads for X (sequential, quotable), carousels for Instagram (visual, educational).

On algorithmic platforms, the first 1-3 seconds determine viewership. The hook must create curiosity, establish relevance, or trigger emotion immediately. Different platforms require different hook strategies.

A story is a sequence of events. A world is an environment where many stories can take place. Strong world-building enables transmedia expansion because any corner of the world can spawn new narratives.

class_schedule.topic: Social Media Strategy and Community Building
learning_objectives: Develop data-informed social media strategy aligned with organizational goals
Understand community management principles beyond content posting
Evaluate social media metrics and distinguish vanity metrics from meaningful KPIs
Design content strategies that build genuine community rather than passive audience
readings: Jab, Jab, Jab, Right Hook by Gary Vaynerchuk, Ch. 2-3
Killing Marketing by Pulizzi and Rose, Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media is not just a distribution channel -- it is a relationship engine. Effective strategy goes beyond posting schedules and hashtags to building genuine community and measurable outcomes. The difference between an audience and a community is engagement direction: an audience watches your content passively; a community interacts with you and with each other actively. Building community requires a fundamentally different approach from broadcasting content. Today we learn to develop social media strategies that serve organizational goals while genuinely serving the community. We start with content pillars -- the 3-5 thematic categories that define what your brand talks about -- and build outward to posting cadence, engagement practices, crisis communication, and measurement. We examine the metrics that actually matter: engagement rate (are people interacting?), save rate (is content valuable enough to revisit?), share rate (is content worth spreading?), and conversion (does social media drive the actions you need?). These meaningful metrics contrast with vanity metrics -- follower count, total impressions, total likes -- that look impressive in reports but do not predict business outcomes.
key_concepts: Content pillars: 3-5 thematic categories aligning brand voice with audience interests, ensuring consistency with variety
Engagement rate vs reach vs impressions: engagement = interaction/followers, reach = unique viewers, impressions = total views including repeats
Community management: moderation policies, response time standards (under 1 hour for complaints), crisis communication protocols
The marketing funnel on social: awareness (reach), consideration (engagement), conversion (clicks/purchases), advocacy (shares/UGC)
Paid vs organic strategy: organic reach declining (2-5% on Facebook), paid amplification for reach, organic for community depth
Content calendar: planning content 2-4 weeks ahead while leaving room for reactive/timely content, balancing pillar distribution
Social listening and sentiment analysis: monitoring brand mentions, industry conversations, and competitor activity for strategic insight
User-generated content (UGC): encouraging and curating audience-created content that builds community ownership and provides social proof
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditing a real brand's social media presence: calculating engagement rates, identifying content pillar distribution, evaluating community health, and drafting a 30-day improvement plan with KPI targets
setup_requirements: Social media analytics data (provided case study)

review.value: Total meaningful engagements (likes + comments + shares + saves) / total followers, as percentage. More valuable than raw follower count because it measures active relationship rather than passive following.
3-5 recurring thematic categories (e.g., behind-the-scenes, educational tips, user spotlights, industry news, product features). Provide strategic consistency while preventing content monotony.
Vanity: follower count, total impressions, total likes (look good, predict nothing). Actionable: engagement rate by content type, save/share ratio, click-through to website, conversion from social.

An audience of 100K passive followers is less valuable than a community of 5K active participants who comment, share, create content, and advocate. Strategy should optimize for community health, not audience size.

class_schedule.topic: Media Ethics, Intellectual Property, and Misinformation
learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks to media production and distribution decisions
Understand copyright, fair use, Creative Commons, and intellectual property for content creators
Identify misinformation and disinformation tactics and evaluate countermeasures
Navigate the ethical complexities of AI-generated content and deepfakes
readings: Media Ethics: Issues and Cases by Patterson and Wilkins, Ch. 5-6
Weaponized Lies by Daniel Levitin, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: With the power to publish comes the responsibility to do so ethically. In an era where anyone can reach millions with a smartphone, the ethical frameworks that once applied primarily to journalists now apply to every content creator. Today we examine the frameworks guiding media practitioners in decisions about truth, ownership, and harm. We cover copyright law as it applies to content creation -- what you can and cannot use, how fair use works (and how often it is misunderstood), and how Creative Commons licenses enable sharing while protecting creators. We examine misinformation and disinformation -- the difference matters because intent determines the appropriate response. And we confront the newest frontier: AI-generated content and deepfakes that blur the line between real and fabricated in ways that challenge every existing ethical framework. The ability to create photorealistic fake video of anyone saying anything is not a future concern -- it is a present reality that media professionals must navigate.
key_concepts: Ethical frameworks: utilitarianism (greatest good for greatest number), deontology (duty-based rules), virtue ethics (what would a person of good character do?), applied to media decisions
Copyright: exclusive rights, duration (life + 70 years), automatic protection, registration benefits, international treaties (Berne Convention)
Fair use four-factor test: purpose and character of use, nature of copyrighted work, amount and substantiality used, effect on market value
Creative Commons licenses: CC-BY (attribution), CC-BY-SA (share-alike), CC-BY-NC (non-commercial), CC0 (public domain dedication)
Misinformation vs disinformation vs malinformation: false/unintentional, false/intentional, true/intended to harm -- different responses for each
Deepfakes and AI-generated content: technical capabilities, detection challenges, ethical implications, and regulatory proposals
Media literacy: critical evaluation skills for identifying manipulated content, evaluating source credibility, and recognizing rhetorical manipulation
Platform responsibility: content moderation, Section 230 protections, and the debate over platform liability for user-generated content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: evaluating a real media ethics dilemma using multiple frameworks, then auditing a piece of viral content for misinformation indicators using a structured checklist
setup_requirements: Access to fact-checking databases (Snopes, PolitiFact, Full Fact)

review.value: (1) Purpose: transformative use favored. (2) Nature: factual works more usable than creative. (3) Amount: less is better, but quality matters (using the 'heart' of a work weighs against). (4) Market effect: does your use substitute for the original?

Misinformation: false content shared without harmful intent (sharing outdated medical advice sincerely). Disinformation: deliberately created and spread to deceive (propaganda, fake news). Malinformation: genuine information shared to cause harm (doxxing).

A set of standardized licenses allowing creators to specify how their work may be used. CC-BY requires only attribution. CC-BY-NC prohibits commercial use. CC0 releases all rights. Understanding licenses prevents both piracy and over-restriction.

Current detection methods: facial inconsistencies, unnatural blinking, lighting mismatches, audio-visual sync issues. But detection capabilities lag behind generation capabilities, making media literacy and source verification essential.

learning_objectives: Apply principles of visual communication to media content creation
Understand photography, video, and graphic design fundamentals for media practitioners
Create visually compelling content that communicates effectively across platforms
Evaluate the relationship between visual choices and message interpretation
readings: Visual Communication: Images with Messages by Paul Martin Lester, Ch. 3-5
Photography composition and visual storytelling basics (provided reading)

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual communication is the fastest and most emotionally powerful form of media messaging. Research consistently shows that visual content receives 94% more views than text-only content, and audiences process visual information 60,000 times faster than text. For media practitioners, visual literacy is not optional -- it is the primary language of digital media platforms. Today we develop the visual communication skills that every media professional needs, regardless of specialization. We cover composition principles (rule of thirds, leading lines, framing, depth), color psychology (how color choices influence emotional response and message interpretation), typography in media (how font choices communicate authority, playfulness, urgency, or calm), and the visual grammar of different platforms (Instagram's square aesthetic, TikTok's authentic roughness, LinkedIn's professional polish). We practice creating visual content that communicates specific messages, evaluating existing visual media for its communication effectiveness, and understanding how visual choices shape audience interpretation.
key_concepts: Composition: rule of thirds, leading lines, framing, depth of field, negative space, and their psychological effects
Color psychology in media: blue (trust, corporate), red (urgency, passion), green (health, growth), yellow (optimism, caution) -- cultural context matters
Typography as communication: serif fonts (authority, tradition), sans-serif (modern, clean), script (personal, elegant), display (bold, attention-grabbing)
Platform visual grammar: Instagram (curated, aspirational), TikTok (authentic, raw), LinkedIn (professional, informative), YouTube (thumbnail-driven, high-contrast)
Visual hierarchy in media content: guiding viewer attention through size, color, contrast, and placement in static and video content
Photo editing ethics: the line between enhancement (exposure correction, cropping) and manipulation (removing/adding elements, body modification)
Video composition: shot types (wide, medium, close-up, extreme close-up), camera movement types, and their narrative purposes
Infographic design: combining data visualization with editorial design to communicate complex information visually
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a visual content package for a hypothetical brand across three platforms: Instagram carousel, TikTok cover frame, and LinkedIn article header, applying platform-specific visual grammar
setup_requirements: Camera or smartphone for photo exercises
Basic photo/video editing software (Canva, CapCut, or Adobe Express)

review.value: Dividing the frame into a 3x3 grid and placing key elements at intersections or along lines. Creates more dynamic and engaging compositions than centering, which can feel static.

Each platform has visual expectations. Instagram rewards polished, cohesive aesthetics. TikTok rewards authentic, unpolished content. LinkedIn rewards professional, clean visuals. Violating platform grammar reduces engagement.

Color influences emotional response before conscious processing. Financial services use blue (trust). Food brands use red/yellow (appetite stimulation). Health brands use green (wellness). Context and cultural associations modify these effects.

Enhancement (adjusting exposure, cropping for composition) is standard practice. Manipulation (removing elements, adding false details, altering body proportions) is ethically problematic, especially in journalism and advertising.

class_schedule.topic: Audio Media: Podcasting and Audio Storytelling
learning_objectives: Understand the podcast landscape and audio media consumption trends
Design podcast concepts with clear audience, format, and value proposition
Apply audio storytelling principles: pacing, sound design, interview technique
Evaluate podcast distribution, discovery, and monetization strategies
readings: Out on the Wire by Jessica Abel, Ch. 1-4
Edison Research: Infinite Dial report on podcast consumption

lecture_notes.introduction: Podcasting has exploded from a niche medium to a mainstream media channel with over 500 million listeners globally. Unlike most digital media that demands visual attention, podcasting captures attention during activities that other media cannot reach: commuting, exercising, cooking, cleaning. This intimacy -- a voice speaking directly into your ears during personal moments -- creates uniquely strong parasocial relationships between hosts and audiences. Today we examine podcasting as both a media studies subject and a practical skill. We analyze what makes successful podcasts succeed: clear value proposition, consistent format, strong hosting voice, effective pacing, and strategic distribution. We also practice the core production skills: interview technique (asking better questions, active listening, following unexpected threads), audio storytelling (using sound design, music, and silence to create emotion), and episode structure (cold open, introduction, segments, call-to-action). The podcasting landscape is increasingly competitive, so we also discuss discovery challenges, monetization models, and the emerging role of AI in podcast production and distribution.
key_concepts: Podcast landscape: 4 million+ shows globally, but only 500K active (posted episode in last 90 days). Discovery is the primary challenge.
Format types: interview, narrative storytelling, panel discussion, solo commentary, hybrid -- each with different production requirements and audience expectations
Audio storytelling principles: pacing (varying rhythm prevents monotony), sound design (ambient sound creates place), silence (strategic pauses create emphasis)
Interview technique: open-ended questions, active listening, follow-up questions that explore unexpected threads, avoiding leading questions
Episode structure: cold open (hook), introduction (set expectations), content segments, conclusion, call-to-action (subscribe, review, share)
Distribution strategy: RSS-based distribution to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Google Podcasts; submit to directories; cross-promote
Monetization: advertising (CPM model), sponsorship (integrated reads), premium content (subscriber exclusives), merchandise, live events
AI in podcasting: automated transcription, AI-generated show notes, voice cloning concerns, algorithmic recommendation on Spotify
lecture_notes.demonstration: Producing a 5-minute podcast segment in class: recording an interview with proper technique, editing for pacing and clarity, adding intro music and sound design, and exporting for distribution
setup_requirements: USB microphone or smartphone with recording app
Audio editing software (Audacity free, or GarageBand)

review.value: Unlike social media where algorithms push content to users, podcasts require active search by listeners. Discovery strategies: guest appearances on established shows, social media clips, newsletter features, SEO-optimized show notes.
Cost Per Mille -- payment per 1,000 downloads. Standard podcast ad rates: $15-25 CPM for pre-roll, $20-30 for mid-roll. A show with 10,000 downloads per episode earns $200-300 per ad placement.

The interview skill of genuinely engaging with what the guest says and following interesting threads rather than rigidly sticking to prepared questions. The best podcast moments come from unexpected follow-up questions.

Podcasts create unusually strong parasocial bonds because audio is processed in brain regions associated with personal conversation. Listeners feel they 'know' podcast hosts personally, creating loyalty beyond other media.

learning_objectives: Develop video content strategies for YouTube, TikTok, and other platforms
Understand video production fundamentals: framing, lighting, audio, editing
Create platform-specific video content that leverages each platform's algorithm and audience
Evaluate video analytics to optimize content strategy
readings: YouTube Secrets by Sean Cannell, Ch. 3-5
TikTok Creator Best Practices guide (official documentation)

lecture_notes.introduction: Video is the dominant media format of the 2020s. YouTube reaches 2.7 billion monthly users. TikTok is the most downloaded app globally. Instagram and Facebook have pivoted heavily toward video content through Reels and in-feed video. Understanding video content strategy and basic production skills is essential for any media professional. Today we cover the strategic and practical dimensions of video content creation. Strategically, we examine how platform algorithms determine video distribution: YouTube rewards watch time and session duration, TikTok rewards completion rate and engagement, Instagram Reels rewards saves and shares. These algorithmic preferences directly shape optimal content strategy for each platform. Practically, we learn the production fundamentals that separate amateur content from professional: proper framing and composition, three-point lighting basics, clean audio capture (more important than video quality for viewer retention), and editing for pacing and platform format. We practice creating content in both long-form (YouTube) and short-form (TikTok/Reels) formats.
key_concepts: Platform algorithm priorities: YouTube (watch time, click-through rate), TikTok (completion rate, replays, shares), Instagram Reels (saves, shares, follows from Reels)
Video production fundamentals: rule of thirds framing, three-point lighting (key, fill, back), clean audio capture, b-roll for visual variety
Short-form vs long-form strategy: TikTok/Reels (15-60 seconds, immediate hook, vertical) vs YouTube (8-20 minutes, SEO-driven, horizontal)
Thumbnail and title strategy: YouTube thumbnails as the most important marketing asset (faces, contrast, curiosity gap), search-optimized titles
Video editing principles: cut on action, L-cuts and J-cuts for natural conversation flow, pacing variation, retention graph analysis for identifying drop-off points
YouTube SEO: keyword research, description optimization, tag strategy, chapter markers, closed captions for accessibility and search
Content series and consistency: building audience expectation through regular posting schedule and recognizable format
Video analytics: retention curves (where viewers stop watching), traffic sources (search, suggested, browse, external), and audience demographics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a 60-second vertical video (TikTok/Reel format) and a 3-minute horizontal video (YouTube format) from the same source material, demonstrating platform-specific editing strategies
setup_requirements: Smartphone or camera for video recording
Video editing software (CapCut, DaVinci Resolve free, or iMovie)
Lighting setup (ring light or window light)
Platform analytics examples for strategy discussion

review.value: A graph showing the percentage of viewers still watching at each point in the video. A sharp drop at 30 seconds means your hook failed. Gradual decline is normal. Spikes indicate moments viewers replay.
Percentage of people who see your thumbnail and click to watch. YouTube benchmark: 4-10% is good. Below 2% means your thumbnail and title are not compelling enough for the topic's search volume.
Research shows viewers will tolerate low video quality but abandon content with poor audio within seconds. Invest in audio capture (even a $20 lavalier mic) before investing in camera upgrades.

Short-form video hooks: question ('Did you know...?'), contradiction ('Everything you know about X is wrong'), demonstration (start with the result, then show the process), and direct address ('Stop scrolling if...').

learning_objectives: Distinguish between traditional advertising and content marketing approaches
Design content marketing strategies that provide genuine audience value
Understand the relationship between brand storytelling and audience trust
Evaluate content marketing effectiveness using appropriate metrics
readings: Content Inc. by Joe Pulizzi, Ch. 3-5
Harvard Business Review: 'The Art of Purposeful Storytelling'

lecture_notes.introduction: Content marketing represents a fundamental shift in how brands communicate: instead of interrupting audiences with advertising, brands create content that audiences actively seek out because it provides genuine value. Red Bull does not advertise energy drinks -- it produces extreme sports content that its audience watches voluntarily. HubSpot does not advertise marketing software -- it publishes marketing education that its audience uses to improve their skills. This approach works because it builds trust, demonstrates expertise, and creates ongoing relationships rather than transactional encounters. Today we examine content marketing strategy, brand storytelling techniques, and the measurement frameworks that distinguish effective content from expensive vanity projects. We also discuss the ethical dimensions: when does content marketing cross the line from providing value to manipulating trust? When brand-funded content mimics independent journalism or hides its commercial intent, it exploits the trust it built.
key_concepts: Content marketing vs advertising: providing value to earn attention vs paying to interrupt attention
Brand storytelling framework: brand purpose (why we exist), brand narrative (our story), content pillars (what we talk about), tone of voice (how we sound)
Content funnel: awareness content (blog, social, video) -> consideration content (guides, comparisons, webinars) -> decision content (case studies, trials, demos)
SEO-driven content strategy: keyword research, search intent matching, content clustering, and the role of authority in search rankings
Thought leadership: establishing brand expertise through original research, opinion, and educational content that advances the conversation
Content distribution: owned (website, email, social), earned (press, shares, mentions), paid (promoted content, sponsorships)
Measurement: content marketing ROI through organic traffic growth, lead generation, email list growth, and attribution to revenue
Ethics of branded content: disclosure requirements, maintaining editorial independence, the native advertising transparency problem
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing a content marketing strategy for a hypothetical brand: defining audience, content pillars, funnel stages, distribution channels, and measurement KPIs
setup_requirements: SEO keyword research tool access (Ubersuggest free tier)

review.value: Content marketing works when the audience would consume the content even without knowing the brand behind it. If removing the brand logo would make the content worthless, it is advertising disguised as content.
The reason behind a search query: informational (how to...), navigational (brand name), commercial investigation (best X for Y), transactional (buy X). Content must match intent to rank and convert.

Great content attracts audience -> audience shares content -> shares attract links and authority -> authority improves search rankings -> rankings attract more audience. Self-reinforcing growth cycle.

Branded content that mimics editorial content must be clearly labeled as sponsored/paid. Failure to disclose violates FTC guidelines (US), ASA rules (UK), and erodes trust when audiences discover the deception.

learning_objectives: Develop crisis communication plans for organizational media emergencies
Apply crisis response frameworks to real-time social media situations
Understand the dynamics of viral negative attention and online reputation damage
Design monitoring systems that detect emerging crises before they escalate
readings: Crisis Communications by Kathleen Fearn-Banks, Ch. 1-3
Case Studies in Crisis Communication (provided compilation)

lecture_notes.introduction: In the age of social media, a crisis can go from local incident to global trending topic in minutes. A single employee's inappropriate tweet, a product failure captured on video, or a mishandled customer complaint can generate millions of impressions of negative brand association before the organization even knows there is a problem. Crisis communication is the discipline of managing these situations: detecting them early, responding appropriately, containing damage, and recovering trust over time. Today we study crisis communication through case studies of both well-handled and catastrophically mismanaged crises. We learn the established frameworks: the 'golden hour' of initial response, the hierarchy of stakeholder communication, the principles of authentic acknowledgment versus defensive denial, and the long-term reputation recovery process. We also examine the unique dynamics of social media crises: the speed of escalation, the mob dynamics of pile-on behavior, the challenge of responding to emotional audiences with measured communication, and the role of screenshots that make every statement permanent.
key_concepts: Crisis types: product failure, employee misconduct, data breach, social media backlash, executive scandal, activist targeting, misinformation campaign
The golden hour: the first 60 minutes of a crisis when response sets the narrative. Silence in the golden hour is filled by others, usually unfavorably.
Crisis response framework: acknowledge, empathize, take responsibility where appropriate, communicate action, follow up with transparency
Stakeholder priority: employees first (they are your messengers), then customers, then media, then general public
Social media crisis dynamics: speed of spread, screenshot permanence, pile-on behavior, context collapse, amplification by outrage algorithms
Crisis plan components: monitoring systems, response team roles, pre-approved statement templates, escalation procedures, post-crisis review
Reputation recovery: consistency of improved behavior over time, not a single apology statement. Trust is rebuilt through actions, not words.
Crisis simulation: practicing response under pressure to build muscle memory for real events
lecture_notes.demonstration: Crisis simulation exercise: teams receive a simulated social media crisis scenario in real-time and must draft responses, manage stakeholder communication, and present their strategy under time pressure

review.value: The first 60 minutes when the narrative is being set. Organizations that respond with acknowledged concern within the golden hour control the story. Those that wait have the story told about them by others.

When content intended for one audience reaches an entirely different audience (an internal memo going viral, a joke for friends seen by customers). Every digital communication should be assumed to be public.

Attempting to suppress information often amplifies it. Demanding removal of a critical post can generate 100x more attention than the original post would have received. Sometimes the best response is no response.

'We apologize for our mistake and here is what we are doing to fix it' (genuine). 'We are sorry if anyone was offended' (non-apology that deflects responsibility). Audiences detect and punish non-apologies.

learning_objectives: Understand the regulatory frameworks governing media in major jurisdictions
Analyze the ongoing debates around platform regulation and content moderation
Evaluate the implications of AI regulation for media production and distribution
Engage with media policy debates as informed practitioners
readings: Digital Media Law by Ashley Packard, Ch. 8-10
EU Digital Services Act summary and analysis (provided)

lecture_notes.introduction: Media regulation is in a period of unprecedented change. Frameworks designed for broadcast television and print newspapers are being applied to platforms that operate nothing like traditional media. Section 230 of the US Communications Decency Act, which shields platforms from liability for user-generated content, is under bipartisan pressure for reform. The EU's Digital Services Act creates new obligations for content moderation, algorithmic transparency, and advertising disclosure. AI regulation is emerging globally, with the EU AI Act establishing risk-based frameworks that will affect media production tools. Understanding these regulatory frameworks matters because they determine what content can be created, how it can be distributed, what disclosures are required, and what liability creators and platforms bear.
key_concepts: Section 230: US law shielding platforms from liability for user content. 'No provider or user of an interactive computer service shall be treated as the publisher or speaker of any information provided by another information content provider.'
EU Digital Services Act: risk-based obligations for platforms including content moderation transparency, algorithmic audit rights, and advertising disclosure requirements
EU AI Act: categorizing AI systems by risk level, with specific requirements for high-risk applications including transparency obligations for AI-generated content
Content moderation models: platform self-regulation, government regulation, co-regulation, and their trade-offs for free expression and harm prevention
Advertising regulations: FTC disclosure requirements for sponsored content, platform-specific ad policies, political advertising transparency
Data protection: GDPR (EU), CCPA (California), and their implications for audience analytics and targeted advertising
Media ownership regulations: cross-ownership limits, concentration concerns, and the debate over platform monopoly power
Freedom of expression: First Amendment (US), Article 10 ECHR (Europe), and the boundary between protected speech and harmful content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy debate exercise: teams take opposing positions on a current media regulation proposal (e.g., requiring algorithmic transparency for content recommendation systems), presenting evidence-based arguments

review.value: The 26 words that created the internet: platforms are not liable for user content AND can moderate content without becoming liable for everything they miss. Reform proposals risk the moderation incentive.
EU regulation requiring very large platforms (45M+ EU users) to conduct algorithmic audits, provide ad transparency, enable researcher access to data, and protect minors. Effective February 2024.

Emerging regulations requiring AI-generated content to be labeled as such. The EU AI Act mandates disclosure for deepfakes and AI-generated text presented as human-authored. Enforcement remains challenging.

Too little regulation allows harm (misinformation, exploitation, monopoly). Too much regulation chills free expression and innovation. Finding the balance is the central challenge of media policy in the digital age.

learning_objectives: Analyze how media systems differ across political, economic, and cultural contexts
Design media content for international audiences with cultural sensitivity
Understand media imperialism, cultural hybridity, and glocalization
Navigate language, cultural, and regulatory differences in global media distribution
readings: Comparing Media Systems by Hallin and Mancini, Ch. 1-2
Global Communication by Thomas McPhail, Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Media does not operate the same way everywhere. The internet may be global, but media systems, audience expectations, regulatory environments, and cultural contexts vary dramatically across countries and regions. A social media strategy that works in the United States may fail completely in Japan, not because the platform is different but because the cultural relationship with brands, humor, self-expression, and public discourse is fundamentally different. Today we examine global media from multiple angles: how political systems shape media environments (authoritarian control vs liberal press freedom), how cultural values influence media consumption and creation (individualist vs collectivist, high-context vs low-context communication), and how global media flows create both cultural exchange and cultural imperialism. We discuss glocalization -- the practice of adapting global content for local markets -- and the skills required for cross-cultural media production.
key_concepts: Media system models: liberal (US/UK, market-driven, low state intervention), democratic corporatist (Nordic, strong public broadcasting, organized self-regulation), polarized pluralist (Mediterranean, political parallelism, state intervention)
Cultural dimensions affecting media: individualism/collectivism (content framing), uncertainty avoidance (tolerance for ambiguity), power distance (authority representation)
High-context vs low-context communication: Japan (implicit, contextual meaning) vs US (explicit, direct meaning) and implications for content creation
Media imperialism: the historical dominance of Western (especially American) media content and its cultural homogenization effects
Cultural hybridity: how local cultures do not simply absorb global media but remix and reinterpret it (K-pop, Bollywood, Nollywood as global forces)
Glocalization: adapting global content strategy for local markets (McDonald's menus, Netflix regional content, social media platform preferences by country)
Language and localization: translation vs transcreation, cultural references that do not travel, humor that is culturally specific
Regulatory variation: content restrictions (China's Great Firewall, EU's right to be forgotten), advertising rules, data protection standards
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cross-cultural content analysis: examining how the same global brand adapts its social media content across US, Japan, Brazil, and Saudi Arabia markets, identifying cultural adaptation strategies
setup_requirements: Global social media examples from multiple markets

review.value: 'Think global, act local.' Adapting a global brand or content strategy to local cultural contexts while maintaining core brand identity. Netflix commissioning local-language content for each market is a prime example.

Cultures where meaning is embedded in context, relationships, and nonverbal cues rather than explicit words. Japanese advertising often communicates through atmosphere and emotion rather than direct product claims.

The dominance of one culture's media over others, potentially eroding local cultural identity. Hollywood's global dominance has historically shaped global beauty standards, storytelling conventions, and cultural values.

Going beyond translation to recreate content for a different cultural context while maintaining the intended emotional impact and meaning. A humor-based US campaign might need an entirely different approach for a Japanese audience.

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Media: AI, Immersive Media, and the Future
learning_objectives: Evaluate the impact of AI tools on media production, distribution, and consumption
Analyze immersive media formats (VR, AR, interactive) and their communication potential
Assess the ethical implications of emerging media technologies
Develop personal frameworks for adapting to continuous technological change
readings: The AI Revolution in Media (provided industry report)
New Media Technologies readings (provided compilation)

lecture_notes.introduction: The media landscape is being reshaped by technologies that would have seemed science-fictional a decade ago. AI can now generate photorealistic images, write coherent text, produce music, clone voices, and create video -- all from text prompts. Immersive media (VR, AR, mixed reality) creates new storytelling possibilities where audiences are participants rather than spectators. Interactive media blurs the line between content creation and consumption as audiences shape narratives through their choices. Today we examine these emerging technologies not as futurism but as present-day tools that are already affecting media practice. AI writing assistants are used in newsrooms, AI image generators illustrate articles, AI voice synthesis narrates podcasts, and AI recommendation systems determine what media 90% of audiences consume. Understanding these tools -- their capabilities, their limitations, their biases, and their ethical implications -- is essential for every media professional. We also develop personal frameworks for continuous adaptation: the specific technologies will change, but the ability to evaluate new tools critically, adopt them appropriately, and maintain ethical standards throughout is a permanent professional skill.
key_concepts: AI content generation: text (GPT), images (DALL-E, Midjourney), audio (voice synthesis), video (Sora, RunwayML) -- capabilities, quality, and limitations
AI in media workflows: research assistance, draft generation, editing automation, transcription, translation, personalization at scale
Immersive media: VR storytelling (presence, embodiment), AR journalism (data overlay on real world), interactive documentary (audience-directed narrative)
AI ethics in media: disclosure obligations, bias in training data, job displacement, intellectual property of AI-generated content
Deepfake detection and media authentication: provenance tracking, content credentials, watermarking, and the arms race between generation and detection
Algorithmic journalism: automated reporting of structured data (sports scores, financial results), and the debate over AI-generated editorial content
Creator tool democratization: AI lowering production barriers (anyone can create professional-quality content), implications for media professionals
Framework for technological adaptation: evaluate capabilities honestly, assess ethical implications, integrate thoughtfully, maintain human editorial judgment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on AI media tool exploration: using text, image, and audio AI tools to produce a media content package, then critically evaluating the output for quality, bias, and ethical implications
setup_requirements: Access to AI content generation tools (ChatGPT, Canva AI, or similar)

review.value: AI tools amplify human capability but do not replace human judgment, creativity, ethics, or accountability. The media professional's role evolves from content creation to content direction, curation, and quality assurance.
Emerging standards (C2PA) that embed provenance metadata into media files, allowing verification of origin, editing history, and AI generation status. A potential solution to the authenticity crisis.

AI tools lower production barriers, enabling more voices. But they also enable more noise, more misinformation at scale, and more competition for attention. Quality curation becomes more valuable, not less.
For any new technology: (1) What can it do? (2) What can it NOT do? (3) What should it do? (4) What should it NOT do? Answering all four prevents both blind adoption and reflexive rejection.

learning_objectives: Present a comprehensive media analysis or production project demonstrating course competencies
Synthesize media theory, practice, and ethics into a personal media philosophy
Evaluate how course frameworks apply to emerging media challenges
Articulate professional readiness for media-related careers
readings: Review all course materials for comprehensive synthesis
Peer project materials for critique preparation

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together fifteen weeks of learning through student presentations and collaborative synthesis. Each student or team presents their capstone project -- whether a cross-platform media analysis, a content strategy, a media production package, or a critical research paper -- and receives feedback from peers and the instructor. Beyond the presentations, we engage in collective synthesis: how do McLuhan's ideas apply to TikTok? How does Hall's encoding/decoding model explain the divergent reactions to AI-generated content? How do the ethical frameworks we studied help us navigate deepfakes, algorithmic bias, and platform power? The media landscape will continue to change rapidly, but the analytical frameworks, ethical commitments, and practical skills you have developed in this course provide a durable foundation. You can now analyze media systems rather than simply consuming them, create content with strategic intention rather than guessing, evaluate ethical dilemmas with principled frameworks rather than intuition, and adapt to new technologies with critical evaluation rather than either fear or uncritical adoption. These are the skills of a media-literate professional.
key_concepts: Capstone presentation: demonstrating integration of theory, practice, and ethics in a comprehensive project
Media philosophy: your personal framework for ethical, effective media practice
Theory application: connecting foundational frameworks (McLuhan, Hall, Jenkins) to contemporary media phenomena
Future readiness: frameworks for evaluating and adapting to media technologies that do not yet exist
Professional identity: the kind of media practitioner you aspire to be and the principles that guide your work
Continuous learning: media literacy as ongoing practice, not a course outcome -- staying critical and current
Media responsibility: the power to shape public discourse creates the obligation to do so ethically
Community contribution: how you will contribute to healthier media ecosystems in your professional practice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student project presentations with structured peer feedback, followed by group synthesis discussion connecting course themes to current media challenges

review.value: Media literacy is not a state to achieve but a practice to maintain. The landscape changes continuously, and the analytical skills must be exercised regularly to remain sharp and relevant.

McLuhan's medium-is-the-message explains why the same content performs differently across platforms. Hall's encoding/decoding explains why audiences interpret the same content differently. Jenkins' convergence explains why content flows across platforms.

In a media environment where attention is currency and outrage drives engagement, the commitment to truth, fairness, and audience respect is both a professional obligation and a competitive advantage.

The specific platforms, tools, and formats will change. The ability to analyze systems, understand audiences, create compelling content, and maintain ethical standards will not. Invest in the permanent skills.

quizzes.topic: Media Theory, Digital Landscape, and Audience Analysis

questions.question: McLuhan argued 'the medium is the message.' Using TikTok as a contemporary example, explain what this means and evaluate whether his framework applies to algorithmic content distribution.
questions.answer: McLuhan meant the medium's form shapes society more than content transmitted through it. TikTok's short-form vertical video format has changed how information is consumed regardless of content: 60-second constraint reshapes attention spans, algorithmic For You Page shapes collective culture, swipe interaction creates disposable content relationship. Framework still applies because the algorithm itself is part of the medium, filtering and prioritizing content in ways that shape understanding independently of creator intent.

questions.question: Define psychographic segmentation and explain why it would be more useful than demographic segmentation for marketing a narrative puzzle game.
questions.answer: Psychographic segmentation divides audiences by psychological characteristics: values, interests, attitudes, personality, lifestyle. Demographic divides by observable characteristics: age, gender, income. For a narrative puzzle game, psychographics are more useful because the audience is defined by interests (puzzle-solving, story-driven experiences) and attitudes (preference for single-player, patience for complex narratives) rather than age or income. Two 35-year-old professionals may have different gaming preferences, but people who enjoy escape rooms and mystery novels across all demographics are likely prospects.

questions.question: What are network effects and how do they create barriers to entry for competing platforms?
questions.answer: Network effects: a platform becomes more valuable as more users join, creating self-reinforcing growth. More users attract more creators, which attracts more users. Barriers: a new competitor starts with zero users and zero content, making it less valuable. Users face switching costs (social graphs, content history, audience locked into existing platform). Examples: Facebook's social graph, YouTube's creator ecosystem.

quizzes.topic: Storytelling, Social Media Strategy, and Ethics

questions.question: Henry Jenkins defines transmedia storytelling as distinct from adaptation. Explain the difference and give an example where two platforms each contribute unique narrative value.
questions.answer: Adaptation retells the same story in a different medium (novel to film). Transmedia distributes different story elements across platforms where each adds unique value. Star Wars example: films tell the core saga, Clone Wars series explores character arcs (Ahsoka) that could not fit in films, Jedi: Fallen Order game provides player-agency narrative between Episodes III-IV, and novels give perspective from ordinary soldiers that deepens the universe without duplicating film content.

rubric.key: Unique contribution of at least two platforms

questions.question: Explain the difference between misinformation, disinformation, and malinformation. Why does the distinction matter for response strategy?
questions.answer: Misinformation: false content shared without harmful intent (sharing outdated medical advice sincerely). Disinformation: deliberately created and spread to deceive (state-sponsored propaganda). Malinformation: genuine information shared to cause harm (doxxing, revenge sharing). Distinction matters because responses differ: misinformation requires correction and education, disinformation requires platform intervention and counter-messaging, malinformation requires legal and platform policy enforcement. Treating all false content as intentional deception alienates sincere but mistaken sharers.

rubric.key: Explains why conflating them is harmful

questions.question: What is the fair use four-factor test? Apply it to the scenario of using 30 seconds of a popular song in a YouTube video essay about music theory.
questions.answer: Four factors: (1) Purpose: educational/commentary use is favored, likely transformative. (2) Nature: the song is a creative work (less favorable). (3) Amount: 30 seconds of a 3-4 minute song is substantial but not the whole work. (4) Market effect: does the essay substitute for the original? Unlikely -- no one watches a music theory essay instead of buying the song. Analysis: factors 1 and 4 favor fair use, factor 2 weighs against, factor 3 is neutral. Overall: likely fair use for educational commentary, especially if the analysis transforms the clip's purpose.

rubric.key: Applies each factor to the scenario

quizzes.topic: Visual Communication, Audio Media, and Video Strategy

questions.question: A podcast has 5,000 downloads per episode and a CPM rate of $25 for mid-roll ads. How much revenue does one mid-roll ad placement generate per episode? If the host wants to earn $3,000/month from ads with weekly episodes, how many ad slots per episode are needed?
questions.answer: Revenue per placement: (5,000 / 1,000) * $25 = $125 per episode per ad. Monthly goal: $3,000. Weekly episodes = 4 per month. Revenue needed per episode: $3,000 / 4 = $750. Ad slots needed: $750 / $125 = 6 mid-roll ad slots per episode. This is excessive and would likely drive listeners away. Better approach: grow audience to 20,000+ downloads or add premium subscriber content.

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'visual grammar' specific to different social media platforms. How does Instagram's visual grammar differ from TikTok's?
questions.answer: Visual grammar: the unwritten conventions for visual content on each platform that audiences expect and algorithms reward. Instagram: curated, polished aesthetic. Cohesive color palettes, professional photography, careful composition. Grid planning for profile appearance. Aspirational lifestyle imagery. TikTok: authentic, unpolished. Smartphone-quality video, direct-to-camera talking, trending sounds and formats, green screen effects. Over-produced content feels out of place and performs poorly because it violates the platform's informal social contract.

questions.question: Why is audio quality more important than video quality for viewer retention in online video content?
questions.answer: Research shows viewers will tolerate low-resolution video (720p vs 4K is barely noticed in most contexts) but will stop watching within 10-15 seconds if audio is unclear, echoey, or has background noise. This is because audio carries the primary information in most content (speech, narration) and poor audio requires cognitive effort to parse, causing fatigue and abandonment. The brain processes visual information more tolerantly because it fills in missing detail, but cannot compensate for garbled or noisy audio.

quizzes.topic: Content Marketing, Crisis Communication, and Regulation

questions.question: Explain the 'golden hour' in crisis communication and describe what an organization should do during that first hour when a negative story goes viral on social media.
questions.answer: The golden hour: the first 60 minutes of a crisis when the narrative is being established. If the organization does not respond, others fill the vacuum with speculation, criticism, and misinformation. Actions: (1) Acknowledge awareness of the situation publicly within 30 minutes, even if details are incomplete. (2) Express concern for affected parties. (3) Commit to investigating and providing updates. (4) Designate a single spokesperson. (5) Begin internal fact-gathering to inform the next statement. Do NOT: speculate about causes, blame others, or minimize the situation.

questions.question: What is Section 230 of the US Communications Decency Act and why is it significant for the current media landscape?
questions.answer: Section 230 states: 'No provider of an interactive computer service shall be treated as the publisher of information provided by another information content provider.' It shields platforms from liability for user-generated content while allowing them to moderate without becoming liable for everything they miss. Significance: without Section 230, platforms would either have to moderate everything (impossible at scale) or moderate nothing (cesspool). It is foundational to how the internet works, and proposed reforms risk either of these extremes. Both political parties want reform but for opposite reasons (Republicans want less moderation, Democrats want more).

rubric.key: Explains dual protection (liability shield + moderation permission)

questions.question: What is the difference between content marketing and native advertising? Why does the distinction matter ethically?
questions.answer: Content marketing: brand-created content that provides genuine, standalone value to audiences (educational blog posts, entertaining videos). The brand is transparent as the creator. Native advertising: paid content designed to look like editorial content on the platform where it appears (sponsored articles that mimic news format). Ethically distinct because native advertising mimics trusted editorial context to gain credibility it did not earn. When a 'news article' is actually a paid placement, readers who do not notice the disclosure are deceived about the source's independence and motivation.

quizzes.topic: Global Media, AI, and Emerging Technologies

questions.question: Explain the concept of glocalization in media and give a specific example of a global media company adapting content for a local market.
questions.answer: Glocalization: adapting global content or brand strategy for local cultural contexts while maintaining core identity. 'Think global, act local.' Example: Netflix commissions local-language original content for each major market (Squid Game for Korea, Dark for Germany, Sacred Games for India) rather than simply dubbing American shows. This respects local storytelling traditions, employs local talent, and creates content that resonates culturally while still being distributed on a global platform.

rubric.key: Explains how adaptation respects local culture

questions.question: Evaluate this statement: 'AI will replace media professionals within 10 years.' Argue both for and against, then state your position with reasoning.
questions.answer: For: AI can already generate text, images, audio, and video. Automated journalism covers sports and finance. AI tools lower production costs dramatically. Against: AI lacks genuine understanding, ethical judgment, cultural sensitivity, and creative vision. It generates plausible content but cannot evaluate truth, nuance, or impact. Human editorial judgment, relationship building, and accountability cannot be automated. My position: AI will transform media roles, not eliminate them. Journalists will shift from writing to editing/verifying AI drafts. Designers will shift from creating to directing AI generation. The media professional who understands both AI capabilities and human editorial judgment will be more valuable, not less.

rubric.key: Clear personal position with nuanced reasoning

questions.question: What is the Content Credentials (C2PA) standard and why is it important for the future of media trust?
questions.answer: C2PA (Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity): a technical standard that embeds tamper-evident metadata into media files recording their origin, editing history, and whether AI was involved in generation. Important because as AI-generated media becomes indistinguishable from real media, audiences need a way to verify authenticity. C2PA allows a photo to carry a verified chain of custody from camera to publication, letting viewers confirm it has not been manipulated. Without such standards, trust in all visual media erodes because anything could be fabricated.

rubric.key: Explains the trust problem it addresses
Why it matters for media's future

major_assignments.title: Cross-Platform Media Analysis Report
major_assignments.description: Select a current media event, brand campaign, or cultural phenomenon and analyze how it operates across at least 3 media platforms. Apply media theory frameworks to evaluate how platform affordances shape message, audience reception, and cultural impact.
objectives: Apply media theory to real-world content analysis across multiple platforms
Demonstrate understanding of platform-specific affordances and constraints
Evaluate audience engagement using both quantitative metrics and qualitative analysis
Synthesize findings into a coherent analytical argument with practical implications
deliverables: 3,500-4,500 word analytical report with APA citations
Platform comparison matrix (affordances, format, audience behavior, metrics)
Audience analysis with at least one research-based persona
Visual appendix with annotated screenshots from each platform
Theoretical framework application section connecting analysis to course readings

criteria: Correctly applies at least 3 media theory frameworks from course readings
Theory integrated into analysis rather than summarized separately
Demonstrates understanding of framework strengths and limitations
Original analytical insights beyond surface-level observation
Thorough analysis of content on each platform with specific examples
Identifies platform affordances shaping content form and audience behavior
Compares engagement patterns across platforms with data
Includes quantitative metrics where available
Goes beyond demographics to psychographic and behavioral analysis
Engagement patterns explained with theoretical reasoning
Persona is research-based and specific enough to guide strategy
Considers multiple audience segments with different readings
Clear thesis and logical argument structure
Proper APA citation throughout
Professional writing quality
Visual appendix well-organized and annotated
Sophisticated application of media theory to real-world analysis
Thorough comparative platform analysis with supporting data
Nuanced audience understanding with research-based personas
Professional writing and presentation quality

major_assignments.title: Content Strategy and Media Production Package
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete content strategy for a real or hypothetical organization, then produce a multi-platform content package demonstrating the strategy. The package must include content for at least 3 platforms with platform-native design, a social media calendar, audience analysis, and measurement framework.
objectives: Design a comprehensive content strategy grounded in audience research and organizational goals
Produce platform-native content that demonstrates visual, audio, and written communication skills
Build a measurement framework that connects content performance to organizational outcomes
Present the strategy and content in a format suitable for professional stakeholder review
deliverables: Content strategy document (8-10 pages): audience analysis, content pillars, platform strategy, tone of voice, measurement KPIs
Produced content package: 3 social media posts, 1 blog article (800+ words), 1 short-form video (30-60 seconds), 1 long-form audio or video segment (3-5 minutes)
30-day content calendar with posting schedule, content types, and pillar distribution
Measurement dashboard design showing KPIs for each platform
Strategy presentation (15-20 slides with speaker notes)

criteria: Audience analysis is research-based with specific personas and psychographic insights
Content pillars are clearly defined and aligned with both audience interests and organizational goals
Platform strategy is differentiated with platform-native approaches rather than cross-posting
Measurement framework connects content metrics to organizational outcomes
Social media content follows platform-specific best practices and visual grammar
Written content is engaging, well-structured, and audience-appropriate
Video content demonstrates basic production quality (framing, audio, editing)
All content reflects the defined brand voice and content pillars
KPIs are meaningful (not vanity metrics) and connected to strategy objectives
Dashboard design is clear and would enable data-driven decision making
Success criteria defined for each content type and platform
Feedback loops specified for iterating strategy based on performance data
Strategy presentation is clear, professional, and persuasive
Slides support the narrative without overwhelming with text
Speaker notes demonstrate depth beyond what is shown on slides
Would convince a skeptical stakeholder to approve the strategy
Comprehensive content strategy grounded in audience research and organizational alignment
Multi-platform content demonstrating production skills across formats
Meaningful measurement framework connecting performance to outcomes
Professional presentation quality suitable for stakeholder approval

major_assignments.title: Media Analysis Research Paper or Critical Project
major_assignments.description: Conduct an in-depth investigation of a media studies topic through either a research paper (academic analysis of a media phenomenon) or a critical project (media production with accompanying critical essay). The work must demonstrate advanced application of course theory, ethical analysis, and original insight.
objectives: Demonstrate advanced media analysis using multiple theoretical frameworks
Produce original insight through rigorous investigation of a media topic
Apply ethical reasoning to media production, distribution, or consumption issues
Present findings in a format that contributes to understanding of the chosen topic
deliverables: Research paper (4,000-5,000 words) OR critical project (media production + 2,500-word critical essay)
Annotated bibliography of 12+ academic and industry sources
Presentation summarizing key findings and their implications (10-15 minutes)

criteria: Multiple theoretical frameworks applied with sophistication, not just summarized
Theory used as analytical tools that generate insight, not decorative citations
Engages with tensions and limitations of chosen frameworks
Demonstrates mastery of course concepts through application
Investigation reveals insights not obvious from casual observation
Arguments are well-supported with evidence from primary and secondary sources
Acknowledges complexity and avoids oversimplification
Contributes meaningfully to understanding of the topic
Ethical dimensions of the topic are identified and analyzed using course frameworks
Multiple perspectives are considered fairly before reaching conclusions
Practical implications for media practitioners are articulated
Personal ethical position is stated and defended
Writing is clear, well-organized, and engages the reader
Citations are thorough and properly formatted (APA)
Presentation communicates key findings effectively
Professional quality suitable for academic or industry audience
Advanced theoretical application demonstrating media analysis mastery
Original insights supported by rigorous evidence and reasoning
Thoughtful ethical analysis with practical implications
Professional writing and presentation quality

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing media theory, digital landscape knowledge, platform analysis, ethical reasoning, and applied media skills

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises in audience analysis, content creation, social media auditing, and media production across formats

major_assignments.description: Three comprehensive projects: cross-platform analysis, content strategy package, and research paper or critical project

midterm_examination.description: Mixed format exam covering media theory, platform economics, audience analysis, ethics, and applied media strategy

final_project.description: Capstone research paper or critical project demonstrating integrated media analysis competency

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions, group presentations, and guest speaker events. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work penalized 10% per day. Extensions may be granted with 48 hours advance notice and valid reason. Content strategy deadlines are firm due to team dependencies.
course_policies.collaboration: Group projects require documented equal contribution. Individual assignments must be your own analysis and writing. Proper citation of all sources is required throughout.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, fabrication of data, and submitting AI-generated text as your own analysis will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board. AI tools may be used for research assistance if disclosed.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop or tablet for in-class activities, internet access, social media accounts for observation, and basic content creation tools (smartphone camera, free editing software). No specific software purchases required.
